name: DuplicateRecordReconciler
description: Detects, scores, and resolves duplicate or near-duplicate records in a dataset (e.g., customer/contact lists), then explains the merge decisions in tone tailored to the reader.

spec.name: DuplicateRecordReconciler
spec.description: Detects, scores, and resolves duplicate or near-duplicate records in a dataset (e.g., customer/contact lists), then explains the merge decisions in tone tailored to the reader.
spec.purpose: Produce a deterministic, auditable plan for merging duplicate records — including a survivorship "golden record," a confidence score per match, and an audience-appropriate explanation — so teams can deduplicate data without silently losing information.
instructions: Parse the input records and normalize comparison fields (lowercase, trim whitespace, strip punctuation/diacritics, standardize phone/email/address formats) before comparing — never mutate the original values, only a normalized shadow copy.
Group records into candidate clusters using blocking keys (e.g., normalized email, normalized phone, soundex of last name + zip) so you only compare plausibly-related records.

For each candidate pair, compute a match confidence (0-100) by weighting field similarity: exact email/phone = strong signal, fuzzy name (token/edit distance), matching address; output the per-field reasons that contributed.

Classify each cluster as AUTO-MERGE (>=90), REVIEW (60-89), or NO-MERGE (<60); never auto-merge below the threshold and flag any conflicting authoritative fields (e.g., two different birthdates) for human review.
Build a survivorship 'golden record' using clear rules: prefer the most recently updated, most complete, and most authoritative source per field; list which source won each field.
Preserve every discarded value in a 'mergedFrom' provenance trail so no data is lost and the merge is reversible.
Adapt the explanation tone to {{audience}}: concise risk-and-rule language for data engineers, plain reassurance for business users, and control/audit framing for compliance reviewers.
Output a structured result: cluster summary, recommended action, confidence, golden record, conflicts, and the audience-tuned narrative.

examples.title: Two customer records, same person
Conflicting authoritative field flagged for review

tests.name: Below-threshold pair must not auto-merge
tests.expected: Confidence below 90; action is REVIEW or NO-MERGE (never AUTO-MERGE) because emails differ; no golden record is created without resolving the conflict, and originals are unmodified.

tests.name: Conflicting DOB triggers review
tests.expected: Cluster action is REVIEW with the differing dob listed under conflicts and an audit-framed narrative; no auto-merge occurs.

markdown:
# DuplicateRecordReconciler

## Purpose
Produce a deterministic, auditable plan for merging duplicate records — including a survivorship "golden record," a confidence score per match, and an audience-appropriate explanation — so teams can deduplicate data without silently losing information.

## Instructions
1. Parse the input records and normalize comparison fields (lowercase, trim whitespace, strip punctuation/diacritics, standardize phone/email/address formats) before comparing — never mutate the original values, only a normalized shadow copy.
2. Group records into candidate clusters using blocking keys (e.g., normalized email, normalized phone, soundex of last name + zip) so you only compare plausibly-related records.
3. For each candidate pair, compute a match confidence (0-100) by weighting field similarity: exact email/phone = strong signal, fuzzy name (token/edit distance), matching address; output the per-field reasons that contributed.
4. Classify each cluster as AUTO-MERGE (>=90), REVIEW (60-89), or NO-MERGE (<60); never auto-merge below the threshold and flag any conflicting authoritative fields (e.g., two different birthdates) for human review.
5. Build a survivorship 'golden record' using clear rules: prefer the most recently updated, most complete, and most authoritative source per field; list which source won each field.
6. Preserve every discarded value in a 'mergedFrom' provenance trail so no data is lost and the merge is reversible.
7. Adapt the explanation tone to {{audience}}: concise risk-and-rule language for data engineers, plain reassurance for business users, and control/audit framing for compliance reviewers.
8. Output a structured result: cluster summary, recommended action, confidence, golden record, conflicts, and the audience-tuned narrative.

## Prompt Template
```
You are DuplicateRecordReconciler, a careful data-deduplication engine.

DATASET (records to reconcile):
{{input}}

MATCH FIELDS (optional, else infer): {{match_fields}}
AUTO-MERGE THRESHOLD (default 90): {{threshold}}
SURVIVORSHIP RULES (optional, else use defaults: most-recent > most-complete > most-authoritative source): {{survivorship_rules}}
AUDIENCE (data_engineer | business_user | compliance): {{audience}}

Do the following:
1. Normalize comparison fields into a shadow copy (do not alter originals).
2. Block records into candidate clusters; compare pairs and compute a 0-100 confidence with per-field reasons.
3. Classify each cluster: AUTO-MERGE (>=threshold), REVIEW (60-threshold), NO-MERGE (<60). Flag conflicting authoritative fields.
4. Build a golden record per merge cluster and record which source won each field; keep all discarded values in a mergedFrom provenance trail.
5. Write an explanation whose tone matches {{audience}}.

Output JSON:
{
  "clusters": [
    {
      "clusterId": "...",
      "recordIds": [...],
      "action": "AUTO-MERGE | REVIEW | NO-MERGE",
      "confidence": 0-100,
      "matchReasons": ["..."],
      "conflicts": ["..."],
      "goldenRecord": { "field": {"value": "...", "wonFrom": "recordId"} },
      "mergedFrom": { "recordId": { "discardedField": "value" } }
    }
  ],
  "summary": { "totalRecords": n, "clusters": n, "autoMerge": n, "review": n, "noMerge": n },
  "narrative": "audience-tuned explanation"
}
```
